does: This paper introduces WeightLens and CircuitLens, two methods that extend circuit discovery beyond traditional activation-based analysis. WeightLens interprets neural network features by directly analyzing their learned weights, eliminating the need for external explainer models or datasets while achieving competitive or superior performance on context-independent features. CircuitLens captures how feature activations emerge from interactions between features, building on transcoder-based decomposition of input-dependent and input-invariant components. Together, these methods provide a more systematic and scalable approach to understanding the computational structure of neural networks without relying on manual inspection or isolation of individual features.
connects: This work builds on the circuit discovery tradition in mechanistic interpretability, which seeks to understand neural networks by tracing computational paths. It responds to limitations of prior automated interpretability approaches that analyze features in isolation and depend heavily on external language models and dataset quality. The methods leverage recent advances in transcoders, which decompose feature attribution into interpretable components. The research extends beyond activation-based analysis, which has dominated interpretability work, and connects to broader efforts to move beyond black-box explanations toward systematic, scalable understanding of feature interactions and learned representations in deep networks.
enables: This work enables more scalable and systematic circuit analysis that could apply to realistic models and complex tasks, moving beyond toy problems that have limited prior interpretability research. By removing dependence on external models and datasets, WeightLens and CircuitLens reduce bottlenecks in automated interpretability pipelines. The direct weight-based interpretation approach opens possibilities for understanding what features learn during training and how network architecture choices affect learned representations. Future work could extend these methods to larger models, investigate feature interactions at scale, and potentially enable intervention-based editing of neural networks based on mechanistic understanding, supporting both interpretability and safety applications in increasingly capable AI systems.
